---
title: "Analysis of Widget Performance"
author: "Your Name"
date: 2026-06-28
format:
  html:
    toc: true
bibliography: refs.bib
execute:
  enabled: false
---

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

This document showcases the **Quarto (MIT)** VS Code extension. The prose is
highlighted as Markdown — **bold**, *italic*, `inline code`, a
[link](https://quarto.org), and inline math $E = mc^2$ — while the YAML front
matter above and the embedded code cells below colorize in their own languages.

The literate-programming approach of @knuth1984 motivates this work. As detailed
in @sec-methods, our results (@tbl-results) follow from the area relation in
@eq-area.

## Background

Cross-references and citations both complete from a single `@` list: heading IDs
like @sec-results, labels like @tbl-results, and citekeys like @lamport1994.

# Methods {#sec-methods}

A Python cell — type `@` in the prose above to see `fig-plot` offered as a
cross-reference completion (drawn from this cell's `label:` option):

```{python}
#| label: fig-plot
#| fig-cap: "Widget throughput over time."
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.show()
```

An R cell (colorizes only with the R extension installed):

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics."
summary(cars)
```

The area of a circle is given in @eq-area:

$$
A = \pi r^2
$$ {#eq-area}

# Results {#sec-results}

Benchmark scores are reported in @tbl-results; the typesetting lineage traces to
@lamport1994.

| Widget | Score |
|--------|-------|
| A      | 9.1   |
| B      | 8.7   |

: Benchmark results {#tbl-results}

# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

Place the cursor inside any code cell and press `Ctrl+Enter` to run it (execution
is delegated to your Jupyter / R / Julia extension). Use **Quarto: Preview** for
a live, auto-reloading preview of this document.
